if you're selling sand, a pure commodity, not a good match for product launch. Otherwise, yes, it's a good match. Just though it's a bit of a hobby of mine, keeping track of all the crazy different markets that people do these launches in. I mean, they're just, I can't remember which ones I have down here, but the last one is Tressage. So that's horse ballet. You all know what horse ballet is, right? I didn't. And one time I was on a stage, just after this woman did $160,000 launch in Tressage, and I had no freaking clue what it was. So then I went and I Googled it. Actually, I went to Wikipedia. Is that a verb? Wikipedia? So anyway, I went to Wikipedia, figured out it was the horse ballet. So then I got up on stage. I was so excited that she did this 160 grand. This is a big room. There's 350, 400 people in this room. And I started talking about this woman, how she did this launch in the dressage market. And I was just all excited about this dress. And someone over on the side said, Jeff, Jeff, that's Tressage. Okay, got me, got me. So anyways, it works in all kinds of different markets. And it's because it relies on basic human psychology, and mental triggers. And it gets people excited. It gives you, the launch sequence gives you the time to build in reciprocity, to build in authority, massive authority. I put out these videos of me recently with this launch, of me interviewing people for case studies. Just builds massive, massive authority. Builds commitment consistency. You can build actually community is a really powerful trigger. I mean, there's clearly a strong community here. I mean, it's just otherwise, why else would you put up with the abuse that Dan heaps on you guys? I mean, it's like, and believe me, I'm one of you. I'm part of this community because I'm sitting out there and I've been looking at his stuff for a decade. But communities really, these are all things that you build in your launch sequence that gets people focused on that launch day. And that's how you do $12,000 in a single second. Okay, so what types of products does it work for? Just all kinds of different products. Physical products, marching band accessories, used marching band accessories. I mean, that's a pretty obscure one. Holstein cow embryos. But like I said, it's mostly, it's the most perfect fit for information products. It's been used really good for coaching, selling coaching, high-end coaching, Platinum groups. Any type of live event, tele-seminars, webinars, online services, offline services, consulting, just goes on and on. Very, very adaptable. Okay, so yeah, it's been pretty impactful. Basically, my clients, I have $200 million up here. I'm pretty sure it's at least 300 million, but I haven't actually gone in and documented. I actually got a little lazy once I did $200 million and I stopped documenting it. So I gotta go back and do it. And of course, I only hear from a small fraction of my product launch formula owners that actually do launches by their telling me about their great success. But I know it's over $200 million in launches. Okay, so now I just wanna, that's sort of the big rah-rah show, how awesome it is. Now, just wanna give you a little bit about how it works. And then we're gonna go into how I took this and sort of went meta on it and created the quote unquote opportunity. Of course, it was not sold as an opportunity officially, but anyways, so I have this thing I call a sideways sales letter. And so how many of you have, say, ever been to an online sales letter? Anyone here's seen an online sales letter? Okay, so obviously we all have. And so what happens when you go to an online sales letter? It's like, a lot of times when you land on it, you don't know you're gonna land on that sales letter. You know, someone snuck a link to you via email and they said this is the most awesome thing I've ever seen and click on it or whatever. Somehow you just land, you click on an ad, you land on that sales letter. Okay, so when that page first loads, there's that scroll bar off on the side there, over there, that little blue thing. That scroll bar, when it page first loads, it's like really big and then it gets smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller. As that page loads, as that 50 page sales letter loads. And we can do 50 page sales letters, right? Because it doesn't cost us anything to print them. So we can do those nice, long sales letters. The reality is that when most people hit those sales letters, they take that scroll bar and scroll it right down to the bottom and they check out your prices. And they don't read the sales letter, right? Either that or they just click away. The sales letters, I mean, I love sales letters, I've written sales letters, I've written sales letters that have done many millions of dollars. I don't know if I can say tens of millions, but multiple millions. I like reading them. I bet you a lot of you guys like reading them too. I mean, they're like, it's a hobby, right? It's cool, because we're into it, but most people aren't reading them, okay? And especially online, they're not terribly effective in terms of closing. I still use them. They're great, but they're not the most effective way to close. So I do this thing, what I call conceptually, this is one of the best teaching and selling tools I came up with, this idea of the sideways sales letter. Sort of a big idea. Instead of being 50 pages or 30 pages or 20 pages long, I said, oh, what if I turn it sideways and I basically tell the same story and do the same thing you would do with a sales letter and I tell it over, say, 10 days or 12 days. So what does a sales letter start with? There you go. It starts with a headline. And then typically, what do you guys like to do after a headline? Subhead. Double readership path, okay. I typically, I'll go into a story pretty quickly after the headline. I'll try to pull them in with a story. And then usually the story will start to evolve into an opportunity, how to make your life better. And this is the opportunity I'm using in a bigger term, but how to make your life better, how to get thinner, how to get in better shape, how to get the love of your life, how to make more money, how to play guitar better, how to play golf better. There's usually some opportunity for them to change their lives. So typically the way I'll do a sales letter, headline, story, or at least set the hook for a story, then an opportunity, then usually build out the story, and then eventually you get down there, you'll talk up bullet points, benefits, get down further, and you start to develop the offer.
